label: design_angles

request_messages.content: You are a mathematical modeling expert specializing in industrial scheduling, operations research, and flexible job-shop scheduling problems (FJSSP). You will help formulate a rigorous mathematical model for a multi-workshop overhaul scheduling problem with TWO equipment crews jointly servicing five workshops, with inter-workshop transportation. The model must inherit two innovations from the single-crew predecessor (Question 2): asynchronous operation-level start times within each process, and early equipment release.

I am working on Question 3 of the 2026-51MCM Problem B competition.


## Problem Statement (Question 3)

Using the equipment of BOTH Crew 1 AND Crew 2, complete the overhaul tasks for
all five workshops A, B, C, D, and E. Formulate a mathematical model to
compute the minimum time required to complete all tasks. Then, in Table 3,
record for each piece of equipment: the equipment ID, start time, end time,
continuous operation duration, process ID, and the crew to which it belongs.

This is a migration from Question 2 (single-crew) to a two-crew shared
equipment pool. The Q2 modeling innovations must be preserved:
- Asynchronous operation-level start times: when a process needs two equipment
  types, the two equipment operations may start at DIFFERENT times.
- Early equipment release: each equipment unit is released at its own
  operation-end time, NOT at the process completion time of its slowest
  co-worker.

Two-crew specifics:
- A process requiring two equipment types may use units from DIFFERENT crews.
- Each equipment unit's initial location depends on the crew it belongs to:
  Crew 1 units start at the Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units start at the Crew 2
  base. The two bases have different distances to each workshop.
- After leaving the base, all subsequent travel between workshops depends only
  on the inter-workshop distance, regardless of crew affiliation.

## Process Flow Data for All Five Workshops

### Workshop A (Process order: A1 -> A2 -> A3)

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload |
|---------|------|--------------------------------|----------|
| A1 | Defect Filling | Precision Filling Machine 200 m³/h AND Automated Conveying Arm 250 m³/h | 300 m³ |
| A2 | Surface Leveling | High-speed Polishing Machine 100 m³/h AND Industrial Cleaning Machine 250 m³/h | 500 m³ |
| A3 | Strength Testing | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 100 m³/h | 500 m³ |

### Workshop B (Process order: B1 -> B2 -> B3 -> B4)

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload |
|---------|------|--------------------------------|----------|
| B1 | Surface Cleaning | Industrial Cleaning Machine 100 m³/h | 120 m³ |
| B2 | Base Layer Construction | Precision Filling Machine 200 m³/h AND Automated Conveying Arm 300 m³/h | 1500 m³ |
| B3 | Surface Sealing | Precision Filling Machine 350 m³/h | 360 m³ |
| B4 | Surface Leveling | High-speed Polishing Machine 120 m³/h AND Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 100 m³/h | 360 m³ |

### Workshop C (Process order: C1 -> C2 -> [C3 -> C4 -> C5] x 3)

Note: Processes C3, C4, C5 are repeated three times. Expand the chain as:
C1 -> C2 -> C3_1 -> C4_1 -> C5_1 -> C3_2 -> C4_2 -> C5_2 -> C3_3 -> C4_3 -> C5_3.

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload (per round) |
|---------|------|--------------------------------|----------|
| C1 | Old Coating Removal | Industrial Cleaning Machine 250 m³/h AND Automated Conveying Arm 250 m³/h | 720 m³ |
| C2 | Base Filling | Precision Filling Machine 350 m³/h | 720 m³ |
| C3 | Sealing Coverage | Precision Filling Machine 200 m³/h AND Automated Conveying Arm 250 m³/h | 360 m³ |
| C4 | Surface Grinding | High-speed Polishing Machine 120 m³/h AND Industrial Cleaning Machine 100 m³/h | 400 m³ |
| C5 | Quality Inspection | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 100 m³/h | 400 m³ |

### Workshop D (Process order: D1 -> D2 -> D3 -> D4 -> D5 -> D6)

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload |
|---------|------|--------------------------------|----------|
| D1 | Debris Removal | Industrial Cleaning Machine 250 m³/h | 600 m³ |
| D2 | Base Solidification | Precision Filling Machine 200 m³/h AND Automated Conveying Arm 300 m³/h | 800 m³ |
| D3 | Surface Sealing | Precision Filling Machine 350 m³/h | 450 m³ |
| D4 | Surface Leveling | High-speed Polishing Machine 120 m³/h AND Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 300 m³/h | 1500 m³ |
| D5 | Load-bearing Inspection | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 300 m³/h | 1500 m³ |
| D6 | Edge Trimming | High-speed Polishing Machine 100 m³/h | 700 m³ |

### Workshop E (Process order: E1 -> E2 -> E3)

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload |
|---------|------|--------------------------------|----------|
| E1 | Foundation Treatment | Industrial Cleaning Machine 250 m³/h | 1000 m³ |
| E2 | Surface Sealing | Precision Filling Machine 350 m³/h | 600 m³ |
| E3 | Stability Inspection | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 300 m³/h AND Industrial Cleaning Machine 100 m³/h | 600 m³ |

## Two-Crew Equipment Configuration (32 units total)

### Crew 1 Equipment (16 units)

| Equipment Type | Equipment IDs | Qty | Speed (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automated Conveying Arm | Automated Conveying Arm1-1 ~ 1-4 | 4 | 2 |
| Industrial Cleaning Machine | Industrial Cleaning Machine1-1 ~ 1-5 | 5 | 2 |
| Precision Filling Machine | Precision Filling Machine1-1 ~ 1-5 | 5 | 2 |
| Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine1-1 | 1 | 2 |
| High-speed Polishing Machine | High-speed Polishing Machine1-1 | 1 | 2 |

### Crew 2 Equipment (16 units)

| Equipment Type | Equipment IDs | Qty | Speed (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automated Conveying Arm | Automated Conveying Arm2-1 ~ 2-4 | 4 | 2 |
| Industrial Cleaning Machine | Industrial Cleaning Machine2-1 ~ 2-5 | 5 | 2 |
| Precision Filling Machine | Precision Filling Machine2-1 ~ 2-5 | 5 | 2 |
| Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine2-1 | 1 | 2 |
| High-speed Polishing Machine | High-speed Polishing Machine2-1 | 1 | 2 |

### Combined Equipment Pool (per equipment type)

| Equipment Type | Crew 1 Units | Crew 2 Units | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| Automated Conveying Arm | 4 | 4 | 8 |
| Industrial Cleaning Machine | 5 | 5 | 10 |
| Precision Filling Machine | 5 | 5 | 10 |
| Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| High-speed Polishing Machine | 1 | 1 | 2 |

All equipment travels at speed 2 m/s.

## Distance Table

### Initial Distances from Crew 1 Base

| Origin | Destination | Distance (m) |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Crew 1 | A | 400 |
| Crew 1 | B | 620 |
| Crew 1 | C | 460 |
| Crew 1 | D | 710 |
| Crew 1 | E | 400 |

### Initial Distances from Crew 2 Base

| Origin | Destination | Distance (m) |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| Crew 2 | A | 500 |
| Crew 2 | B | 460 |
| Crew 2 | C | 620 |
| Crew 2 | D | 680 |
| Crew 2 | E | 550 |

### Inter-Workshop Distances (symmetric)

| Origin | Destination | Distance (m) |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| A | B | 1020 |
| A | C | 1050 |
| A | D | 900 |
| A | E | 1400 |
| B | C | 1100 |
| B | D | 1630 |
| B | E | 720 |
| C | D | 520 |
| C | E | 850 |
| D | E | 1030 |

Transport time = distance / speed, where speed = 2 m/s for all equipment.
Initial transport time uses the base-to-workshop distance specific to the
crew the equipment unit belongs to.

## Key Assumptions from Problem Statement

1. The sequence of processes within each workshop is fixed; processes must be
   executed strictly in the given order.
2. If a process requires two different types of equipment, both units must
   independently complete the full workload. The process is complete only
   after both have finished. The two units may belong to different crews and
   may start at DIFFERENT times (asynchronous operation-level start).
3. Equipment can be reused across different processes and workshops, but each
   piece of equipment can serve only one process at a time.
4. Equipment transfer within the same workshop has zero transport time.
   Equipment transfer between different workshops requires non-negligible
   transport time = distance / speed.
5. Start time is 00:00:00 (HH:MM:SS). Durations in seconds, rounded up
   (ceiling).
6. No return to base is required at the end.

## Critical Modeling Innovations Inherited from Question 2

### Innovation 1 — Asynchronous operation-level start times

For a process j requiring equipment types t1 and t2, do NOT introduce a
single shared start variable S_j. Instead, introduce one start variable per
operation:
  s_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2}
The two operations may start at different times. The process completion is
the maximum of the two operation ends:
  PCT_j = max(s_{j,t1} + p_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2} + p_{j,t2})

### Innovation 2 — Early equipment release

Each equipment unit is released at its own operation-end time, not at the
process completion time:
  - Operation Completion Time: e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}
  - Process Completion Time:   PCT_j   = max_{t in E_j} e_{j,t}
  - Equipment Release Time:    R_{j,k,t} = e_{j,t}  (NOT PCT_j)
  - Next-use constraint: s_{j',t} >= e_{j,t} + transport_time(w_j, w_{j'})
    when the same unit k serves both j and j'.

### Innovation 3 — Per-operation precedence (not per-process)

Workshop precedence is enforced per equipment-type operation of the
successor process:
  s_{succ, t} >= PCT_{pred}   for every t in E_{succ}.

The two innovations together allow a faster equipment unit to leave a process
early and travel to its next assignment without waiting for its slower
co-worker.


I plan to analyze this problem from ten distinct modeling angles using ten
parallel agents. Below are my proposed ten angles. Please review, refine if
needed, and output a final confirmed list of exactly ten angles. For each
angle, provide:
- Angle ID (1-10)
- Title
- A detailed description of what this angle should cover (3-5 sentences)

Proposed angles:

1. **Formal Sets, Indices, and Parameter Definitions for the Two-Crew FJSSP**: Provide rigorous mathematical definitions of all sets, indices, and parameters for Question 3. Define the set of workshops W = {A, B, C, D, E}; the expanded set of processes (with Workshop C's C3-C5 chain unrolled three times into C3_1..C5_3); the set of crews G = {1, 2}; the set of equipment types T (5 types); the set of individual equipment units U partitioned by crew U = U_1 ∪ U_2 (16 + 16 = 32 units); the equipment-to-process requirement mapping E_j ⊆ T for each process j; processing times p_{j,t} = ⌈workload_j / efficiency_{j,t} × 3600⌉; the workshop of each process w(j); the crew of each unit g(k); and two distance tables: initial distance δ_g(w) from each crew base g to each workshop w, and inter-workshop distance d(w, w'). Convert all distances to transport times using speed 2 m/s. Pay special attention to indexing conventions distinguishing units of the same type but from different crews.

2. **Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables**: Formulate the operation-level asynchronous start variables that constitute one of the two key Q2 innovations carried into Q3. For each process j and each required equipment type t ∈ E_j, define a start variable s_{j,t} and an end expression e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}. Define the process completion time PCT_j = max_{t ∈ E_j} e_{j,t}. Show with a concrete example (e.g., process A1 needing Precision Filling Machine for 5400 s and Automated Conveying Arm for 4320 s) how the two operations may start at DIFFERENT times while still completing the process correctly. Explicitly reject the naive single shared start S_j formulation. Discuss why this asynchronous model is strictly more flexible than the synchronous one, and why the optimal makespan can only be smaller or equal.

3. **Two-Crew Equipment Assignment with Cross-Crew Sharing**: Model the equipment assignment decision for the combined pool of 32 units (16 per crew). For each operation (j, t), define an assignment binary x_{j,t,k} = 1 if individual unit k ∈ U_t serves operation (j, t), where U_t is the set of all units of type t across BOTH crews. Enforce that exactly one unit is assigned per operation: ∑_{k ∈ U_t} x_{j,t,k} = 1. Critically, the two equipment types of the same process may be served by units from DIFFERENT crews — there is no constraint requiring same-crew co-workers. Discuss bottleneck types: with only 2 High-speed Polishing Machines (one per crew) and 2 Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machines, these single-per-crew types remain critical resources even after the doubling. Analyze how the doubled pool of 8 Automated Conveying Arms and 10 Precision Filling Machines reduces resource contention compared to Q2.

4. **Equipment Path and Crew-Specific Transport-Time Modeling**: Formulate the routing of each equipment unit across workshops, with crew-specific initial positions. For each unit k ∈ U_g (where g = g(k) is the crew of k), the first workshop visit incurs the initial transport time τ_init(k, w) = δ_g(w) / speed. Between consecutive operations on the same unit, the transport time depends only on the workshop pair: τ(w, w') = d(w, w') / speed; if w = w' the transport time is zero. Adopt the path-style direct-successor arc formulation per equipment unit (CP-SAT AddCircuit) introduced in Q2: SOURCE_k → (j, t) arc carries s_{j,t} ≥ τ_init(k, w(j)); (i, t) → (j, t) arc carries s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i), w(j)); (i, t) → SINK_k arc terminates the path; SOURCE_k → SINK_k arc models an unused unit; self-loops at unassigned candidates allow AddCircuit to skip them. Provide the full distance/time table for BOTH bases and verify symmetry of the inter-workshop matrix.

5. **Early Equipment Release Mechanism for the Two-Crew Pool**: Provide the rigorous formalization of the early equipment release mechanism extended to the two-crew pool. For unit k assigned to operation (j, t), define the release time R_{j,k,t} = e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}, NOT PCT_j. The non-overlap and transport constraints between two consecutive operations (i, t) and (j, t) on the same unit k become:
  s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i), w(j))
Crucially, the release time of a faster unit is NOT held back by a slower co-worker in the same process — even when the two co-workers are from different crews. Provide a concrete example: in process A1, if a Precision Filling Machine from Crew 1 takes 5400 s while an Automated Conveying Arm from Crew 2 takes 4320 s, the Conveying Arm is freed at e_{A1, ACA} = 4320 s + (its own start), so it may depart for its next workshop while the Filling Machine is still working. Quantify the makespan reduction this delivers.

6. **Intra-Workshop Precedence and Workshop C Three-Round Expansion**: Formulate intra-workshop precedence using PER-OPERATION constraints (not per-process). For each ordered pair (pred, succ) of consecutive processes in the same workshop, and for each equipment type t ∈ E_succ, enforce:
  s_{succ, t} ≥ PCT_pred
where PCT_pred = max_{t' ∈ E_pred} e_{pred, t'}. This is stronger than enforcing only on the earliest-starting operation of succ. Carefully expand Workshop C's three-round repetition into 11 processes: C1 → C2 → C3_1 → C4_1 → C5_1 → C3_2 → C4_2 → C5_2 → C3_3 → C4_3 → C5_3, each carrying its own (s, e, PCT) variables. Confirm that the workload-per-round in Workshop C means the processing time of each round is identical (e.g., C3_1 = C3_2 = C3_3 in duration), but each round's operations have independent start variables and may use different equipment units.

7. **CP-SAT-Oriented Implementation Design**: Design the variable and constraint structure so the model can be implemented directly in Google OR-Tools CP-SAT. Specify: IntVar for each s_{j,t} with domain [0, H] where H is a horizon upper bound; the linear expression e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t} without introducing a separate variable; AddMaxEquality to bind PCT_j; BoolVar for each x_{j,t,k}; arc literals for the path-style AddCircuit per unit. Detail the AddCircuit construction: for each unit k, build the candidate node set {SOURCE_k, SINK_k, (j, t) for every operation that requires type t(k)}, then build five arc categories (SOURCE → op, op → op, op → SINK, SOURCE → SINK, self-loops), each guarded by its own arc literal. Show OnlyEnforceIf bindings between arc literals and timing constraints. Discuss the makespan variable C_max = max over terminal processes and how to wire it via AddMaxEquality. Provide search-strategy hints (DecisionStrategy, num_search_workers, hinting, symmetry breaking across identical units within the same crew).

8. **MIP / Big-M Alternative Formulation**: Provide a complete mixed-integer programming alternative for solvers that lack CP-SAT's AddCircuit primitive. Use big-M disjunctive sequencing variables y_{i,j,k} ∈ {0,1} for every ordered pair of operations of type t(k) sharing unit k:
  s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i), w(j)) − M(1 − y_{i,j,k})
  s_{i,t} ≥ e_{j,t} + τ(w(j), w(i)) − M·y_{i,j,k}
These are activated only when both operations are assigned to k (i.e., x_{i,t,k} = x_{j,t,k} = 1). Express PCT_j = max_t e_{j,t} via PCT_j ≥ e_{j,t} for each t, and minimize C_max ≥ PCT_terminal. Linearize the conditional initial-transport via:
  s_{j,t} ≥ τ_init(k, w(j)) · z_{j,t,k,first}
where z is a 'first-on-unit' indicator; alternatively, model the initial transport via a single-commodity flow from a virtual depot. Discuss the tradeoffs: the number of big-M sequencing variables grows with the square of operations per type, which is manageable for the doubled but still moderate Q3 instance (≈40-50 operations per common type).

9. **Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules**: Define the exact format of Table 3 as required by the problem statement: columns are Equipment ID, Start Time (HH:MM:SS), End Time (HH:MM:SS), Continuous Operation Duration (seconds), Process ID, and Crew (1 or 2 — derived from the equipment unit's name suffix). Each row corresponds to a SINGLE equipment-operation, so a process j requiring two equipment types yields TWO rows in Table 3, possibly with different Start/End times. State the validation rules explicitly:
1. Assignment correctness: every operation has exactly one row.
2. Asynchronous starts allowed: rows of the same process need NOT share Start Time.
3. Per-row precedence: every row of a successor process satisfies row.Start ≥ PCT_pred, where PCT_pred is the max end-time over the predecessor's rows.
4. PCT consistency: PCT_j equals the max End Time over j's rows.
5. Equipment non-overlap: per Equipment ID, rows do not overlap in time and the gap between consecutive rows is at least the transport time between their workshops.
6. Initial-transport: the first row for each unit must have Start ≥ δ_{g(k)}(w) / speed, where g(k) is the unit's crew.
7. Early-release verification: each row's End Time = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}, independent of the process's PCT.

10. **Modeling Assumptions, Ambiguities, and Edge Cases**: Enumerate all modeling assumptions, identify ambiguities in the problem statement, and document edge cases:
1. Initial transport: Crew 1 units depart from Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units depart from Crew 2 base. The 00:00:00 reference is the time at which both crews begin moving, not the time of first operation.
2. Workshop C repetition: workloads listed are PER ROUND; processing times are repeated for each round but with independent start variables.
3. Asynchronous starts: within-process operations of different types are independent; same-process co-workers may be from different crews and may begin at different times.
4. No preemption: each operation runs without interruption; once started, it occupies its assigned unit for the full p_{j,t}.
5. No splitting: workload per type is indivisible; a single process operation cannot be served by two units of the same type in parallel.
6. No return-to-base: equipment does not need to return to its originating base after the schedule completes.
7. Cross-crew interaction: the two crews' equipment pools are fully fungible; there is no penalty or restriction on assigning a Crew 2 unit to support a Crew 1 unit on the same process.
8. Single-unit-per-crew bottlenecks: the Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine and High-speed Polishing Machine each have only one unit per crew (two total) — analyze whether this remains a binding constraint after the doubling.
9. Distance symmetry: confirm that inter-workshop distances are symmetric; the base-to-workshop distances are NOT symmetric between the two crews (each crew has its own initial-distance table).
10. Time integrality: all durations are integer seconds (ceiling of workload/efficiency × 3600).


Output your response as a structured numbered list. Keep each angle focused
and non-overlapping. Ensure that together, the ten angles cover the complete
mathematical formulation needed for Question 3, with special emphasis on:
- The two-crew equipment pool (32 units) and crew-specific initial positions
- Asynchronous operation-level start times within each process (Q2 innovation)
- Early equipment release: each unit is freed at its own e_{j,t}, not at PCT_j
- Cross-crew sharing within the same process is permitted
- Workshop C's three-round expansion (C3-C5 repeated three times)
- Bottleneck analysis for single-unit-per-crew equipment types
- Table 3 output requirements including the "Crew" column

response_content:
**Confirmed Final List of Ten Modeling Angles**

1. **Formal Sets, Indices, and Parameter Definitions for the Two-Crew FJSSP**  
   Rigorously define all sets, indices, and parameters needed for the two‑crew model. Enumerate the workshops W={A,B,C,D,E} and expand Workshop C into 11 processes (C1, C2, C3_1, C4_1, C5_1, …, C5_3). Specify the crew set G={1,2}, the five equipment types T, and the full equipment pool U=U₁∪U₂ containing exactly 32 individually indexed units. Provide the mapping Eⱼ⊆T for each process, the workload‑to‑duration conversion p_{j,t}=⌈workload/efficiency×3600⌉, the crew function g(k), the workshop function w(j), and two distance matrices: crew‑base‑to‑workshop initial distances δ_g(w) and the symmetric inter‑workshop distances d(w,w′). All distances are converted to transport times using the common speed v=2 m/s.

2. **Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables**  
   Introduce the fundamental decision variables that enable the Q2 innovation: for every process j and every required equipment type t∈Eⱼ, define a continuous start time variable s_{j,t} and compute the operation end time e_{j,t}=s_{j,t}+p_{j,t}. The process completion time is then PCT_j = max_{t∈Eⱼ} e_{j,t}. Contrast this with a naive single‑start‑per‑process formulation and demonstrate with a concrete example (e.g., process A1) that the two operations may begin at different instants while still jointly completing the process. Emphasise that this strictly generalises the synchronous case and can only produce a makespan that is smaller than or equal to that of any forced synchronous schedule.

3. **Two-Crew Equipment Assignment with Cross-Crew Sharing**  
   Model the assignment of individual equipment units to operations using binary variables x_{j,t,k} ∈ {0,1}, where k is drawn from Uₜ, the set of all units of type t irrespective of crew. Impose the convexity constraint ∑_{k∈Uₜ} x_{j,t,k}=1 for every operation (j,t). Permit self‑crossing: the two equipment types of a single process may be served by units belonging to different crews, without any same‑crew co‑worker restriction. Discuss the resource contention of the two bottleneck types (only 2 High‑speed Polishing Machines and 2 Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machines in total) and how the doubling of other types (8 Automated Conveying Arms, 10 Precision Filling Machines, 10 Industrial Cleaning Machines) alleviates pressure relative to the single‑crew case.

4. **Equipment Path and Crew-Specific Transport-Time Modelling**  
   Formulate the sequencing and routing constraints for each equipment unit as it moves through a series of operations. Because the two crews start from different bases, initial travel time depends on the unit’s crew: for the first operation (j,t) assigned to unit k, the start time must satisfy s_{j,t}≥δ_{g(k)}(w(j))/v. Subsequent moves between workshops incur a travel time τ(w(i),w(j))=d(w(i),w(j))/v, with zero travel when staying in the same workshop. Use a path‑based disjunctive formulation (e.g., a circuit model) to enforce that each unit’s operations form a single chain, and that the start time of a succeeding operation respects the end time of the predecessor plus the appropriate travel time. This general path model will later be realised in CP‑SAT (Angle 7) or MIP (Angle 8).

5. **Early Equipment Release Mechanism for the Two-Crew Pool**  
   Formalise the second Q2 innovation: each equipment unit is released immediately after it finishes its own operation, not at the process’s overall completion. For a unit k assigned to operation (j,t), define the release time R_{j,k,t}=e_{j,t}. The non‑overlap and transport constraint between two consecutive assignments of the same unit therefore reads s_{j′,t} ≥ e_{j,t} + τ(w(j),w(j′)). Provide a concrete illustration (e.g., in process A1 a fast Automated Conveying Arm from Crew 2 can depart after 4320 s while the Precision Filling Machine from Crew 1 still runs for 5400 s) and quantify how this early departure reduces overall makespan. Stress that cross‑crew co‑workers do not delay each other’s release.

6. **Intra-Workshop Precedence and Workshop C Three-Round Expansion**  
   Enforce the fixed intra‑workshop process sequences using per‑operation‑type precedence. For every consecutive pair (pred, succ) in a workshop and for every equipment type t∈E_succ, impose s_{succ,t} ≥ PCT_pred. Because PCT_pred = max_{t′∈E_pred} e_{pred,t′}, a successor operation of any type cannot start until the entire predecessor process is finished. Expand the three rounds of Workshop C into the explicit chain C1 → C2 → C3_1 → C4_1 → C5_1 → C3_2 → C4_2 → C5_2 → C3_3 → C4_3 → C5_3, each with its own operation‑level variables and identical processing times for the corresponding round‑repeated processes, but independent start decisions and potentially different equipment assignments.

7. **CP-SAT-Oriented Implementation Design**  
   Map the abstract mathematical model to Google OR‑Tools CP‑SAT primitives. Specify IntVar domains for every s_{j,t} (lower bound 0, upper bound Horizon H), and compute e_{j,t} as a linear expression. Use AddMaxEquality to bind each PCT_j. Define BoolVar for assignment x_{j,t,k} and arc literals for the per‑unit circuit AddCircuit. Build SOURCE→operation, operation→operation, operation→SINK, SOURCE→SINK, and self‑loop arcs, guarding each arc’s timing constraint with OnlyEnforceIf. Introduce the makespan variable C_max = max over terminal processes and wire it into the objective. Discuss symmetry‑breaking across identical units of the same crew, search strategy hints (DecisionStrategy, num_search_workers), and the use of initial solution hints to accelerate solution.

8. **MIP / Big‑M Alternative Formulation**  
   Derive a complete mixed‑integer programming formulation that does not rely on CP‑SAT’s global constraints. Introduce disjunctive sequencing variables y_{i,j,k} for every ordered pair of operations that use the same equipment type, coupled with assignment variables x_{i,t,k}. For unit k, impose s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i),w(j)) − M(1−y_{i,j,k}) and the symmetric constraint when the order is reversed, activated through linearisations of the product y_{i,j,k} and x_{i,t,k}·x_{j,t,k}. Model the crew‑specific initial travel by a first‑operation indicator or a virtual‑depot flow. Linearise PCT_j = max e_{j,t} by simple inequalities PCT_j ≥ e_{j,t}. State the objective as minimise C_max ≥ PCT for all terminal processes. Comment on the manageable size of the resulting MIP for this 32‑unit instance.

9. **Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules**  
   Specify the exact structure of the required Table 3: columns Equipment ID, Start Time (HH:MM:SS), End Time, Continuous Operation Duration (seconds), Process ID, and Crew (derived from the equipment name suffix). Clarify that a two‑equipment process produces two rows, possibly with different start/end times. Enumerate seven validation rules: (1) every operation has exactly one row; (2) rows of the same process need not share a start time; (3) every successor‑process row satisfies row.Start ≥ PCT_pred; (4) PCT_j equals the maximum End Time among its rows; (5) per Equipment ID, rows do not overlap and inter‑row gaps cover at least the transport time; (6) the first row for each unit respects crew‑base initial travel; (7) each row’s End Time equals its own start plus its own processing time, independent of the co‑worker’s duration, verifying the early release principle.

10. **Modeling Assumptions, Ambiguities, and Edge Cases**  
    Compile an exhaustive list of assumptions and clarifications. Document that both crews begin moving from their respective bases at t=0, and that Workshop C workloads are per‑round with processing times repeated but independent. Confirm that asynchronous starts and cross‑crew assignments are allowed, that operations are non‑preemptive and unsplittable, and that equipment never returns to base. Analyse the two single‑unit‑per‑crew bottlenecks (Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machine and High‑speed Polishing Machine) and discuss whether they still constrain the doubled pool. Verify the symmetry of inter‑workshop distances and the asymmetry of the two base‑distance tables, and ensure all time values are integer seconds obtained via ceiling of (workload/efficiency×3600).

request_messages.content: You are a mathematical modeling expert. You are one of ten parallel agents, each tackling Question 3 of the 2026-51MCM Problem B from a specific angle. Your assigned angle is Angle 2: Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables. The problem is a two-crew (Crew 1 and Crew 2) multi-workshop FJSSP with asynchronous operation-level start times and early equipment release.

## Your Task

You are Angle 2 agent. Produce a detailed, rigorous mathematical
formulation for Question 3 from the following perspective:

**Angle 2: Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables**
Formulate the operation-level asynchronous start variables that constitute one of the two key Q2 innovations carried into Q3. For each process j and each required equipment type t ∈ E_j, define a start variable s_{j,t} and an end expression e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}. Define the process completion time PCT_j = max_{t ∈ E_j} e_{j,t}. Show with a concrete example (e.g., process A1 needing Precision Filling Machine for 5400 s and Automated Conveying Arm for 4320 s) how the two operations may start at DIFFERENT times while still completing the process correctly. Explicitly reject the naive single shared start S_j formulation. Discuss why this asynchronous model is strictly more flexible than the synchronous one, and why the optimal makespan can only be smaller or equal.

## Problem Data


## Problem Statement (Question 3)

Using the equipment of BOTH Crew 1 AND Crew 2, complete the overhaul tasks for
all five workshops A, B, C, D, and E. Formulate a mathematical model to
compute the minimum time required to complete all tasks. Then, in Table 3,
record for each piece of equipment: the equipment ID, start time, end time,
continuous operation duration, process ID, and the crew to which it belongs.

This is a migration from Question 2 (single-crew) to a two-crew shared
equipment pool. The Q2 modeling innovations must be preserved:
- Asynchronous operation-level start times: when a process needs two equipment
  types, the two equipment operations may start at DIFFERENT times.
- Early equipment release: each equipment unit is released at its own
  operation-end time, NOT at the process completion time of its slowest
  co-worker.

Two-crew specifics:
- A process requiring two equipment types may use units from DIFFERENT crews.
- Each equipment unit's initial location depends on the crew it belongs to:
  Crew 1 units start at the Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units start at the Crew 2
  base. The two bases have different distances to each workshop.
- After leaving the base, all subsequent travel between workshops depends only
  on the inter-workshop distance, regardless of crew affiliation.

## Process Flow Data for All Five Workshops

Transport time = distance / speed, where speed = 2 m/s for all equipment.
Initial transport time uses the base-to-workshop distance specific to the
crew the equipment unit belongs to.

## Key Assumptions from Problem Statement

1. The sequence of processes within each workshop is fixed; processes must be
   executed strictly in the given order.
2. If a process requires two different types of equipment, both units must
   independently complete the full workload. The process is complete only
   after both have finished. The two units may belong to different crews and
   may start at DIFFERENT times (asynchronous operation-level start).
3. Equipment can be reused across different processes and workshops, but each
   piece of equipment can serve only one process at a time.
4. Equipment transfer within the same workshop has zero transport time.
   Equipment transfer between different workshops requires non-negligible
   transport time = distance / speed.
5. Start time is 00:00:00 (HH:MM:SS). Durations in seconds, rounded up
   (ceiling).
6. No return to base is required at the end.

## Critical Modeling Innovations Inherited from Question 2

### Innovation 1 — Asynchronous operation-level start times

For a process j requiring equipment types t1 and t2, do NOT introduce a
single shared start variable S_j. Instead, introduce one start variable per
operation:
  s_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2}
The two operations may start at different times. The process completion is
the maximum of the two operation ends:
  PCT_j = max(s_{j,t1} + p_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2} + p_{j,t2})

### Innovation 2 — Early equipment release

Each equipment unit is released at its own operation-end time, not at the
process completion time:
  - Operation Completion Time: e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}
  - Process Completion Time:   PCT_j   = max_{t in E_j} e_{j,t}
  - Equipment Release Time:    R_{j,k,t} = e_{j,t}  (NOT PCT_j)
  - Next-use constraint: s_{j',t} >= e_{j,t} + transport_time(w_j, w_{j'})
    when the same unit k serves both j and j'.

### Innovation 3 — Per-operation precedence (not per-process)

Workshop precedence is enforced per equipment-type operation of the
successor process:
  s_{succ, t} >= PCT_{pred}   for every t in E_{succ}.

The two innovations together allow a faster equipment unit to leave a process
early and travel to its next assignment without waiting for its slower
co-worker.


## Design Phase Output (for context)

**Confirmed Final List of Ten Modeling Angles**

1. **Formal Sets, Indices, and Parameter Definitions for the Two-Crew FJSSP**  
   Rigorously define all sets, indices, and parameters needed for the two‑crew model. Enumerate the workshops W={A,B,C,D,E} and expand Workshop C into 11 processes (C1, C2, C3_1, C4_1, C5_1, …, C5_3). Specify the crew set G={1,2}, the five equipment types T, and the full equipment pool U=U₁∪U₂ containing exactly 32 individually indexed units. Provide the mapping Eⱼ⊆T for each process, the workload‑to‑duration conversion p_{j,t}=⌈workload/efficiency×3600⌉, the crew function g(k), the workshop function w(j), and two distance matrices: crew‑base‑to‑workshop initial distances δ_g(w) and the symmetric inter‑workshop distances d(w,w′). All distances are converted to transport times using the common speed v=2 m/s.

2. **Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables**  
   Introduce the fundamental decision variables that enable the Q2 innovation: for every process j and every required equipment type t∈Eⱼ, define a continuous start time variable s_{j,t} and compute the operation end time e_{j,t}=s_{j,t}+p_{j,t}. The process completion time is then PCT_j = max_{t∈Eⱼ} e_{j,t}. Contrast this with a naive single‑start‑per‑process formulation and demonstrate with a concrete example (e.g., process A1) that the two operations may begin at different instants while still jointly completing the process. Emphasise that this strictly generalises the synchronous case and can only produce a makespan that is smaller than or equal to that of any forced synchronous schedule.

3. **Two-Crew Equipment Assignment with Cross-Crew Sharing**  
   Model the assignment of individual equipment units to operations using binary variables x_{j,t,k} ∈ {0,1}, where k is drawn from Uₜ, the set of all units of type t irrespective of crew. Impose the convexity constraint ∑_{k∈Uₜ} x_{j,t,k}=1 for every operation (j,t). Permit self‑crossing: the two equipment types of a single process may be served by units belonging to different crews, without any same‑crew co‑worker restriction. Discuss the resource contention of the two bottleneck types (only 2 High‑speed Polishing Machines and 2 Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machines in total) and how the doubling of other types (8 Automated Conveying Arms, 10 Precision Filling Machines, 10 Industrial Cleaning Machines) alleviates pressure relative to the single‑crew case.

4. **Equipment Path and Crew-Specific Transport-Time Modelling**  
   Formulate the sequencing and routing constraints for each equipment unit as it moves through a series of operations. Because the two crews start from different bases, initial travel time depends on the unit’s crew: for the first operation (j,t) assigned to unit k, the start time must satisfy s_{j,t}≥δ_{g(k)}(w(j))/v. Subsequent moves between workshops incur a travel time τ(w(i),w(j))=d(w(i),w(j))/v, with zero travel when staying in the same workshop. Use a path‑based disjunctive formulation (e.g., a circuit model) to enforce that each unit’s operations form a single chain, and that the start time of a succeeding operation respects the end time of the predecessor plus the appropriate travel time. This general path model will later be realised in CP‑SAT (Angle 7) or MIP (Angle 8).

5. **Early Equipment Release Mechanism for the Two-Crew Pool**  
   Formalise the second Q2 innovation: each equipment unit is released immediately after it finishes its own operation, not at the process’s overall completion. For a unit k assigned to operation (j,t), define the release time R_{j,k,t}=e_{j,t}. The non‑overlap and transport constraint between two consecutive assignments of the same unit therefore reads s_{j′,t} ≥ e_{j,t} + τ(w(j),w(j′)). Provide a concrete illustration (e.g., in process A1 a fast Automated Conveying Arm from Crew 2 can depart after 4320 s while the Precision Filling Machine from Crew 1 still runs for 5400 s) and quantify how this early departure reduces overall makespan. Stress that cross‑crew co‑workers do not delay each other’s release.

6. **Intra-Workshop Precedence and Workshop C Three-Round Expansion**  
   Enforce the fixed intra‑workshop process sequences using per‑operation‑type precedence. For every consecutive pair (pred, succ) in a workshop and for every equipment type t∈E_succ, impose s_{succ,t} ≥ PCT_pred. Because PCT_pred = max_{t′∈E_pred} e_{pred,t′}, a successor operation of any type cannot start until the entire predecessor process is finished. Expand the three rounds of Workshop C into the explicit chain C1 → C2 → C3_1 → C4_1 → C5_1 → C3_2 → C4_2 → C5_2 → C3_3 → C4_3 → C5_3, each with its own operation‑level variables and identical processing times for the corresponding round‑repeated processes, but independent start decisions and potentially different equipment assignments.

7. **CP-SAT-Oriented Implementation Design**  
   Map the abstract mathematical model to Google OR‑Tools CP‑SAT primitives. Specify IntVar domains for every s_{j,t} (lower bound 0, upper bound Horizon H), and compute e_{j,t} as a linear expression. Use AddMaxEquality to bind each PCT_j. Define BoolVar for assignment x_{j,t,k} and arc literals for the per‑unit circuit AddCircuit. Build SOURCE→operation, operation→operation, operation→SINK, SOURCE→SINK, and self‑loop arcs, guarding each arc’s timing constraint with OnlyEnforceIf. Introduce the makespan variable C_max = max over terminal processes and wire it into the objective. Discuss symmetry‑breaking across identical units of the same crew, search strategy hints (DecisionStrategy, num_search_workers), and the use of initial solution hints to accelerate solution.

8. **MIP / Big‑M Alternative Formulation**  
   Derive a complete mixed‑integer programming formulation that does not rely on CP‑SAT’s global constraints. Introduce disjunctive sequencing variables y_{i,j,k} for every ordered pair of operations that use the same equipment type, coupled with assignment variables x_{i,t,k}. For unit k, impose s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i),w(j)) − M(1−y_{i,j,k}) and the symmetric constraint when the order is reversed, activated through linearisations of the product y_{i,j,k} and x_{i,t,k}·x_{j,t,k}. Model the crew‑specific initial travel by a first‑operation indicator or a virtual‑depot flow. Linearise PCT_j = max e_{j,t} by simple inequalities PCT_j ≥ e_{j,t}. State the objective as minimise C_max ≥ PCT for all terminal processes. Comment on the manageable size of the resulting MIP for this 32‑unit instance.

9. **Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules**  
   Specify the exact structure of the required Table 3: columns Equipment ID, Start Time (HH:MM:SS), End Time, Continuous Operation Duration (seconds), Process ID, and Crew (derived from the equipment name suffix). Clarify that a two‑equipment process produces two rows, possibly with different start/end times. Enumerate seven validation rules: (1) every operation has exactly one row; (2) rows of the same process need not share a start time; (3) every successor‑process row satisfies row.Start ≥ PCT_pred; (4) PCT_j equals the maximum End Time among its rows; (5) per Equipment ID, rows do not overlap and inter‑row gaps cover at least the transport time; (6) the first row for each unit respects crew‑base initial travel; (7) each row’s End Time equals its own start plus its own processing time, independent of the co‑worker’s duration, verifying the early release principle.

10. **Modeling Assumptions, Ambiguities, and Edge Cases**  
    Compile an exhaustive list of assumptions and clarifications. Document that both crews begin moving from their respective bases at t=0, and that Workshop C workloads are per‑round with processing times repeated but independent. Confirm that asynchronous starts and cross‑crew assignments are allowed, that operations are non‑preemptive and unsplittable, and that equipment never returns to base. Analyse the two single‑unit‑per‑crew bottlenecks (Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machine and High‑speed Polishing Machine) and discuss whether they still constrain the doubled pool. Verify the symmetry of inter‑workshop distances and the asymmetry of the two base‑distance tables, and ensure all time values are integer seconds obtained via ceiling of (workload/efficiency×3600).

## Instructions

1. Write your analysis in well-structured Markdown.
2. Use proper LaTeX math notation ($ ... $ for inline, $$ ... $$ for display).
3. Be rigorous: define all variables, state all assumptions, derive results.
4. This is a multi-workshop problem with the COMBINED equipment pool of Crew 1
   and Crew 2 (32 units total) shared across five workshops A, B, C, D, E.
5. CRITICAL: Each equipment unit's initial location depends on its crew —
   Crew 1 units start at the Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units start at the Crew 2
   base. Subsequent inter-workshop travel uses the shared workshop distance
   matrix.
6. CRITICAL: Inherit the Q2 innovations:
   - Asynchronous starts: for a process j needing types t1, t2, use SEPARATE
     start variables s_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2}; do NOT introduce a single shared
     S_j.
   - Early release: equipment is released at its own operation end e_{j,t}
     = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}, not at PCT_j.
7. Cross-crew sharing within a process is permitted: the two equipment types
   of one process may be served by units from different crews.
8. Pay attention to Workshop C where processes C3->C4->C5 repeat three times.
9. Table 3 must include a "Crew" column derived from the unit's affiliation.
10. Your output will be combined with nine other angles into a final report.
11. Focus ONLY on your assigned angle — do not duplicate other angles' work.
12. Length: aim for 800-1200 words of substantive content.

response_content:
## Angle 2: Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables

This section formulates the core timing variables that enable the **asynchronous operation-level start** innovation inherited from Question 2 and carried into the two‑crew, multi‑workshop Flexible Job‑Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSSP) of Question 3. We explicitly reject the naive “shared start per process” approach and demonstrate the superiority of the per‑operation variables through a concrete example using Workshop A, Process A1.

### 1. Notation and Parameters

Let the set of all processes be $\mathcal{J}$, where a process $j \in \mathcal{J}$ belongs to a specific workshop $w(j) \in \{A,B,C,D,E\}$.  
For each process $j$, the set of required equipment **types** is denoted $E_j \subseteq \mathcal{T}$, with $\mathcal{T} = \{\text{Automated Conveying Arm}, \text{Precision Filling Machine}, \text{Industrial Cleaning Machine}, \text{High‑speed Polishing Machine}, \text{Automatic Sensing Multi‑Function Machine}\}$.

The **processing time** of an operation of type $t \in E_j$ is a known parameter  

\[
p_{j,t} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{workload}_j}{\text{efficiency}_t} \times 3600 \right\rceil \quad\text{(seconds)}.
\]

All durations are positive integers. Example: for Process A1 ($300\,\text{m}^3$),  
- Precision Filling Machine: efficiency $200\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ $\Rightarrow p_{A1,\text{PFM}} = \lceil 300/200 \times 3600 \rceil = 5400$ s.  
- Automated Conveying Arm: efficiency $250\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ $\Rightarrow p_{A1,\text{ACA}} = \lceil 300/250 \times 3600 \rceil = 4320$ s.

### 2. Asynchronous Operation‑Level Variables

For every process $j$ and every required equipment type $t \in E_j$, we introduce a continuous non‑negative decision variable

\[
s_{j,t} \in \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0} ,
\]

the **start time** of the operation using equipment type $t$ at process $j$.  
The **end time** of the same operation is defined directly as

\[
e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}.
\]

Crucially, **no single shared start $S_j$ is defined**. The two (or more) operations of a process may begin at completely different instants. The moment the *entire* process $j$ is finished – i.e., when *all* required operations are complete – is captured by the **process completion time**:

\[
\mathrm{PCT}_j = \max_{t \in E_j} \; e_{j,t}.
\]

### 3. Explicit Rejection of the Naive Synchronous Formulation

A naive model would force synchronous operation starts by introducing a single variable $S_j$ and setting $s_{j,t} = S_j$ for all $t \in E_j$. Then

\[
\mathrm{PCT}_j^{\text{sync}} = S_j + \max_{t \in E_j} p_{j,t},
\]

and every equipment unit is occupied from $S_j$ until $\mathrm{PCT}_j$, regardless of whether its own work finishes earlier. This approach is **rejected** because:

- It ties the release of all co‑working equipment to the slowest operation, wasting potential for early re‑deployment.
- It eliminates the possibility of starting a faster co‑worker later (or a slower one earlier) to better balance the schedule with transport and preceding tasks.
- It is a special case of the asynchronous model (adding equality constraints), thus it cannot yield a better optimal makespan.

### 4. Example: Process A1 with Asynchronous Starts

Consider Process A1 (workshop A) requiring two equipment types:

| Operation | $p_{j,t}$ (s) |
|-----------|----------------|
| Precision Filling Machine (PFM) | 5400 |
| Automated Conveying Arm (ACA)   | 4320 |

**Synchronous schedule:** both start at $S_{A1}=0$, so $\mathrm{PCT}_{A1}=5400$. The ACA finishes at 4320 s but remains “busy” until 5400 s, preventing its use elsewhere for 1080 extra seconds.

**Asynchronous schedule:** the operations may start at different times. Suppose the ACA is finishing a prior assignment in another workshop and can arrive at workshop A only at $t = 1200$ s. The PFM is free earlier. Then we can set

\[
s_{A1,\text{PFM}} = 0,\qquad s_{A1,\text{ACA}} = 1080\;(>1200?\; \text{adjusted}).
\]

In general, to minimise the process finish time, the slowest operation determines the bottleneck. By starting the ACA later, we may synchronise its end with that of the PFM:

\[
e_{A1,\text{PFM}} = 0+5400 = 5400,\qquad e_{A1,\text{ACA}} = 1080+4320 = 5400,
\]

yielding the same $\mathrm{PCT}_{A1}=5400$, but the ACA is free until 1080 s, which can be used to serve another workshop, **reducing the overall makespan**. Any forced synchronous start would either needlessly delay the ACA’s previous job or force the whole process to start later if we insist on a shared $S_j$, harming the global schedule. The asynchronous model captures this flexibility.

### 5. Strict Generality and Optimal Makespan Bound

The set of feasible schedules under the asynchronous formulation strictly contains the synchronous schedules, because the former relaxes the constraint $s_{j,t_1}=s_{j,t_2}$ for all $t_1,t_2 \in E_j$. Consequently, the minimal makespan achievable by the asynchronous model is **less than or equal to** the minimal makespan under any synchronous regime. Formally,

\[
\mathrm{C}_{\max}^{\text{async}} \;\le\; \mathrm{C}_{\max}^{\text{sync}}.
\]

Equality holds only if the optimal asynchronous schedule happens to have all co‑worker starts equal – a very unlikely scenario given the complexity of the two‑crew, 32‑unit sharing problem.

### 6. Connection to Early Equipment Release (Innovation 2)

The asynchronous start variables directly enable the second Q2 innovation: **early equipment release**. Because the release time of an equipment unit assigned to operation $(j,t)$ is defined as its own operation end time:

\[
R_{j,k,t} = e_{j,t},
\]

**not** the process completion time $\mathrm{PCT}_j$. When $s_{j,t_1} \neq s_{j,t_2}$, the faster unit finishes earlier and can depart the workshop sooner. In the A1 example, the ACA would be released at 5400 s (if synchronised) or earlier if it starts earlier, but the PFM would be released at its own end (5400 s). With synchronous starts, the ACA would be forced to wait until the PFM finishes, artificially tying up the unit. Thus operation‑level asynchronous variables are a prerequisite for the early‑release mechanism – together they form the foundation of the scheduling model for Question 3.

### 7. Role in the Global Model

The variables $\{s_{j,t}\}_{j\in\mathcal{J},\,t\in E_j}$ are the primary continuous decision variables of the full mathematical model. They appear in:

- **Process completion** constraints defining $\mathrm{PCT}_j$ via max expressions.
- **Workshop precedence** constraints: for a successor process $j'$, $s_{j',t} \ge \mathrm{PCT}_j$ for every $t \in E_{j'}$.
- **Equipment sequencing** constraints: after an assignment $(j,t,k)$, the next assignment $(j',t)$ on the same unit $k$ must satisfy $s_{j',t} \ge e_{j,t} + \text{transport\_time}(w(j),w(j'))$.
- **Initial travel** constraints incorporating crew‑base distances and the asynchronous starts of the first operations.

All these interactions are elaborated in other Angles (4, 5, 6). The present formulation establishes the variable space and its flexibility.

### 8. Summary

By defining separate start variables $s_{j,t}$ for each equipment type required by a process, we eliminate the artificial coupling that a single $S_j$ would impose. This generalisation is **strictly more flexible**, permits earlier release of fast equipment, and can only improve (or leave unchanged) the optimal makespan. The concrete example of Process A1 illustrates the practical advantage. This asynchronous model is therefore an essential building block of the two‑crew FJSSP formulation, and it will be used throughout all subsequent constraints and solution approaches.

request_messages.content: You are a mathematical modeling expert. You are one of ten parallel agents, each tackling Question 3 of the 2026-51MCM Problem B from a specific angle. Your assigned angle is Angle 9: Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules. The problem is a two-crew (Crew 1 and Crew 2) multi-workshop FJSSP with asynchronous operation-level start times and early equipment release.

## Your Task

You are Angle 9 agent. Produce a detailed, rigorous mathematical
formulation for Question 3 from the following perspective:

**Angle 9: Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules**
Define the exact format of Table 3 as required by the problem statement: columns are Equipment ID, Start Time (HH:MM:SS), End Time (HH:MM:SS), Continuous Operation Duration (seconds), Process ID, and Crew (1 or 2 — derived from the equipment unit's name suffix). Each row corresponds to a SINGLE equipment-operation, so a process j requiring two equipment types yields TWO rows in Table 3, possibly with different Start/End times. State the validation rules explicitly:
1. Assignment correctness: every operation has exactly one row.
2. Asynchronous starts allowed: rows of the same process need NOT share Start Time.
3. Per-row precedence: every row of a successor process satisfies row.Start ≥ PCT_pred, where PCT_pred is the max end-time over the predecessor's rows.
4. PCT consistency: PCT_j equals the max End Time over j's rows.
5. Equipment non-overlap: per Equipment ID, rows do not overlap in time and the gap between consecutive rows is at least the transport time between their workshops.
6. Initial-transport: the first row for each unit must have Start ≥ δ_{g(k)}(w) / speed, where g(k) is the unit's crew.
7. Early-release verification: each row's End Time = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}, independent of the process's PCT.

## Problem Data


## Problem Statement (Question 3)

Using the equipment of BOTH Crew 1 AND Crew 2, complete the overhaul tasks for
all five workshops A, B, C, D, and E. Formulate a mathematical model to
compute the minimum time required to complete all tasks. Then, in Table 3,
record for each piece of equipment: the equipment ID, start time, end time,
continuous operation duration, process ID, and the crew to which it belongs.

This is a migration from Question 2 (single-crew) to a two-crew shared
equipment pool. The Q2 modeling innovations must be preserved:
- Asynchronous operation-level start times: when a process needs two equipment
  types, the two equipment operations may start at DIFFERENT times.
- Early equipment release: each equipment unit is released at its own
  operation-end time, NOT at the process completion time of its slowest
  co-worker.

Two-crew specifics:
- A process requiring two equipment types may use units from DIFFERENT crews.
- Each equipment unit's initial location depends on the crew it belongs to:
  Crew 1 units start at the Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units start at the Crew 2
  base. The two bases have different distances to each workshop.
- After leaving the base, all subsequent travel between workshops depends only
  on the inter-workshop distance, regardless of crew affiliation.

## Process Flow Data for All Five Workshops

### Workshop A (Process order: A1 -> A2 -> A3)

| A2 | Surface Leveling | High-speed Polishing Machine 100 m³/h AND Industrial Cleaning Machine 250 m³/h | 500 m³ |
| A3 | Strength Testing | Automatic Sensing Multi-Function Machine 100 m³/h | 500 m³ |

### Workshop C (Process order: C1 -> C2 -> [C3 -> C4 -> C5] x 3)

Note: Processes C3, C4, C5 are repeated three times. Expand the chain as:
C1 -> C2 -> C3_1 -> C4_1 -> C5_1 -> C3_2 -> C4_2 -> C5_2 -> C3_3 -> C4_3 -> C5_3.

| Process | Name | Equipment Required (efficiency) | Workload (per round) |

## Two-Crew Equipment Configuration (32 units total)

### Crew 1 Equipment (16 units)

### Combined Equipment Pool (per equipment type)

| Industrial Cleaning Machine | 5 | 5 | 10 |
| Precision Filling Machine | 5 | 5 | 10 |

| High-speed Polishing Machine | 1 | 1 | 2 |

All equipment travels at speed 2 m/s.

## Distance Table

### Initial Distances from Crew 1 Base

| Origin | Destination | Distance (m) |

| Crew 1 | B | 620 |
| Crew 1 | C | 460 |

| Crew 1 | E | 400 |

### Initial Distances from Crew 2 Base

| Crew 2 | A | 500 |
| Crew 2 | B | 460 |

| Crew 2 | E | 550 |

### Inter-Workshop Distances (symmetric)

| Origin | Destination | Distance (m) |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| A | B | 1020 |
| A | C | 1050 |
| A | D | 900 |
| A | E | 1400 |
| B | C | 1100 |
| B | D | 1630 |
| B | E | 720 |
| C | D | 520 |
| C | E | 850 |
| D | E | 1030 |

Transport time = distance / speed, where speed = 2 m/s for all equipment.
Initial transport time uses the base-to-workshop distance specific to the
crew the equipment unit belongs to.

## Key Assumptions from Problem Statement

1. The sequence of processes within each workshop is fixed; processes must be
   executed strictly in the given order.
2. If a process requires two different types of equipment, both units must
   independently complete the full workload. The process is complete only
   after both have finished. The two units may belong to different crews and
   may start at DIFFERENT times (asynchronous operation-level start).
3. Equipment can be reused across different processes and workshops, but each
   piece of equipment can serve only one process at a time.
4. Equipment transfer within the same workshop has zero transport time.
   Equipment transfer between different workshops requires non-negligible
   transport time = distance / speed.
5. Start time is 00:00:00 (HH:MM:SS). Durations in seconds, rounded up
   (ceiling).
6. No return to base is required at the end.

## Critical Modeling Innovations Inherited from Question 2

### Innovation 1 — Asynchronous operation-level start times

For a process j requiring equipment types t1 and t2, do NOT introduce a
single shared start variable S_j. Instead, introduce one start variable per
operation:
  s_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2}
The two operations may start at different times. The process completion is
the maximum of the two operation ends:
  PCT_j = max(s_{j,t1} + p_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2} + p_{j,t2})

### Innovation 2 — Early equipment release

Each equipment unit is released at its own operation-end time, not at the
process completion time:
  - Operation Completion Time: e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}
  - Process Completion Time:   PCT_j   = max_{t in E_j} e_{j,t}
  - Equipment Release Time:    R_{j,k,t} = e_{j,t}  (NOT PCT_j)
  - Next-use constraint: s_{j',t} >= e_{j,t} + transport_time(w_j, w_{j'})
    when the same unit k serves both j and j'.

### Innovation 3 — Per-operation precedence (not per-process)

Workshop precedence is enforced per equipment-type operation of the
successor process:
  s_{succ, t} >= PCT_{pred}   for every t in E_{succ}.

The two innovations together allow a faster equipment unit to leave a process
early and travel to its next assignment without waiting for its slower
co-worker.


## Design Phase Output (for context)

**Confirmed Final List of Ten Modeling Angles**

1. **Formal Sets, Indices, and Parameter Definitions for the Two-Crew FJSSP**  
   Rigorously define all sets, indices, and parameters needed for the two‑crew model. Enumerate the workshops W={A,B,C,D,E} and expand Workshop C into 11 processes (C1, C2, C3_1, C4_1, C5_1, …, C5_3). Specify the crew set G={1,2}, the five equipment types T, and the full equipment pool U=U₁∪U₂ containing exactly 32 individually indexed units. Provide the mapping Eⱼ⊆T for each process, the workload‑to‑duration conversion p_{j,t}=⌈workload/efficiency×3600⌉, the crew function g(k), the workshop function w(j), and two distance matrices: crew‑base‑to‑workshop initial distances δ_g(w) and the symmetric inter‑workshop distances d(w,w′). All distances are converted to transport times using the common speed v=2 m/s.

2. **Operation-Level Asynchronous Scheduling Variables**  
   Introduce the fundamental decision variables that enable the Q2 innovation: for every process j and every required equipment type t∈Eⱼ, define a continuous start time variable s_{j,t} and compute the operation end time e_{j,t}=s_{j,t}+p_{j,t}. The process completion time is then PCT_j = max_{t∈Eⱼ} e_{j,t}. Contrast this with a naive single‑start‑per‑process formulation and demonstrate with a concrete example (e.g., process A1) that the two operations may begin at different instants while still jointly completing the process. Emphasise that this strictly generalises the synchronous case and can only produce a makespan that is smaller than or equal to that of any forced synchronous schedule.

3. **Two-Crew Equipment Assignment with Cross-Crew Sharing**  
   Model the assignment of individual equipment units to operations using binary variables x_{j,t,k} ∈ {0,1}, where k is drawn from Uₜ, the set of all units of type t irrespective of crew. Impose the convexity constraint ∑_{k∈Uₜ} x_{j,t,k}=1 for every operation (j,t). Permit self‑crossing: the two equipment types of a single process may be served by units belonging to different crews, without any same‑crew co‑worker restriction. Discuss the resource contention of the two bottleneck types (only 2 High‑speed Polishing Machines and 2 Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machines in total) and how the doubling of other types (8 Automated Conveying Arms, 10 Precision Filling Machines, 10 Industrial Cleaning Machines) alleviates pressure relative to the single‑crew case.

4. **Equipment Path and Crew-Specific Transport-Time Modelling**  
   Formulate the sequencing and routing constraints for each equipment unit as it moves through a series of operations. Because the two crews start from different bases, initial travel time depends on the unit’s crew: for the first operation (j,t) assigned to unit k, the start time must satisfy s_{j,t}≥δ_{g(k)}(w(j))/v. Subsequent moves between workshops incur a travel time τ(w(i),w(j))=d(w(i),w(j))/v, with zero travel when staying in the same workshop. Use a path‑based disjunctive formulation (e.g., a circuit model) to enforce that each unit’s operations form a single chain, and that the start time of a succeeding operation respects the end time of the predecessor plus the appropriate travel time. This general path model will later be realised in CP‑SAT (Angle 7) or MIP (Angle 8).

5. **Early Equipment Release Mechanism for the Two-Crew Pool**  
   Formalise the second Q2 innovation: each equipment unit is released immediately after it finishes its own operation, not at the process’s overall completion. For a unit k assigned to operation (j,t), define the release time R_{j,k,t}=e_{j,t}. The non‑overlap and transport constraint between two consecutive assignments of the same unit therefore reads s_{j′,t} ≥ e_{j,t} + τ(w(j),w(j′)). Provide a concrete illustration (e.g., in process A1 a fast Automated Conveying Arm from Crew 2 can depart after 4320 s while the Precision Filling Machine from Crew 1 still runs for 5400 s) and quantify how this early departure reduces overall makespan. Stress that cross‑crew co‑workers do not delay each other’s release.

6. **Intra-Workshop Precedence and Workshop C Three-Round Expansion**  
   Enforce the fixed intra‑workshop process sequences using per‑operation‑type precedence. For every consecutive pair (pred, succ) in a workshop and for every equipment type t∈E_succ, impose s_{succ,t} ≥ PCT_pred. Because PCT_pred = max_{t′∈E_pred} e_{pred,t′}, a successor operation of any type cannot start until the entire predecessor process is finished. Expand the three rounds of Workshop C into the explicit chain C1 → C2 → C3_1 → C4_1 → C5_1 → C3_2 → C4_2 → C5_2 → C3_3 → C4_3 → C5_3, each with its own operation‑level variables and identical processing times for the corresponding round‑repeated processes, but independent start decisions and potentially different equipment assignments.

7. **CP-SAT-Oriented Implementation Design**  
   Map the abstract mathematical model to Google OR‑Tools CP‑SAT primitives. Specify IntVar domains for every s_{j,t} (lower bound 0, upper bound Horizon H), and compute e_{j,t} as a linear expression. Use AddMaxEquality to bind each PCT_j. Define BoolVar for assignment x_{j,t,k} and arc literals for the per‑unit circuit AddCircuit. Build SOURCE→operation, operation→operation, operation→SINK, SOURCE→SINK, and self‑loop arcs, guarding each arc’s timing constraint with OnlyEnforceIf. Introduce the makespan variable C_max = max over terminal processes and wire it into the objective. Discuss symmetry‑breaking across identical units of the same crew, search strategy hints (DecisionStrategy, num_search_workers), and the use of initial solution hints to accelerate solution.

8. **MIP / Big‑M Alternative Formulation**  
   Derive a complete mixed‑integer programming formulation that does not rely on CP‑SAT’s global constraints. Introduce disjunctive sequencing variables y_{i,j,k} for every ordered pair of operations that use the same equipment type, coupled with assignment variables x_{i,t,k}. For unit k, impose s_{j,t} ≥ e_{i,t} + τ(w(i),w(j)) − M(1−y_{i,j,k}) and the symmetric constraint when the order is reversed, activated through linearisations of the product y_{i,j,k} and x_{i,t,k}·x_{j,t,k}. Model the crew‑specific initial travel by a first‑operation indicator or a virtual‑depot flow. Linearise PCT_j = max e_{j,t} by simple inequalities PCT_j ≥ e_{j,t}. State the objective as minimise C_max ≥ PCT for all terminal processes. Comment on the manageable size of the resulting MIP for this 32‑unit instance.

9. **Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules**  
   Specify the exact structure of the required Table 3: columns Equipment ID, Start Time (HH:MM:SS), End Time, Continuous Operation Duration (seconds), Process ID, and Crew (derived from the equipment name suffix). Clarify that a two‑equipment process produces two rows, possibly with different start/end times. Enumerate seven validation rules: (1) every operation has exactly one row; (2) rows of the same process need not share a start time; (3) every successor‑process row satisfies row.Start ≥ PCT_pred; (4) PCT_j equals the maximum End Time among its rows; (5) per Equipment ID, rows do not overlap and inter‑row gaps cover at least the transport time; (6) the first row for each unit respects crew‑base initial travel; (7) each row’s End Time equals its own start plus its own processing time, independent of the co‑worker’s duration, verifying the early release principle.

10. **Modeling Assumptions, Ambiguities, and Edge Cases**  
    Compile an exhaustive list of assumptions and clarifications. Document that both crews begin moving from their respective bases at t=0, and that Workshop C workloads are per‑round with processing times repeated but independent. Confirm that asynchronous starts and cross‑crew assignments are allowed, that operations are non‑preemptive and unsplittable, and that equipment never returns to base. Analyse the two single‑unit‑per‑crew bottlenecks (Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machine and High‑speed Polishing Machine) and discuss whether they still constrain the doubled pool. Verify the symmetry of inter‑workshop distances and the asymmetry of the two base‑distance tables, and ensure all time values are integer seconds obtained via ceiling of (workload/efficiency×3600).

## Instructions

1. Write your analysis in well-structured Markdown.
2. Use proper LaTeX math notation ($ ... $ for inline, $$ ... $$ for display).
3. Be rigorous: define all variables, state all assumptions, derive results.
4. This is a multi-workshop problem with the COMBINED equipment pool of Crew 1
   and Crew 2 (32 units total) shared across five workshops A, B, C, D, E.
5. CRITICAL: Each equipment unit's initial location depends on its crew —
   Crew 1 units start at the Crew 1 base; Crew 2 units start at the Crew 2
   base. Subsequent inter-workshop travel uses the shared workshop distance
   matrix.
6. CRITICAL: Inherit the Q2 innovations:
   - Asynchronous starts: for a process j needing types t1, t2, use SEPARATE
     start variables s_{j,t1}, s_{j,t2}; do NOT introduce a single shared
     S_j.
   - Early release: equipment is released at its own operation end e_{j,t}
     = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}, not at PCT_j.
7. Cross-crew sharing within a process is permitted: the two equipment types
   of one process may be served by units from different crews.
8. Pay attention to Workshop C where processes C3->C4->C5 repeat three times.
9. Table 3 must include a "Crew" column derived from the unit's affiliation.
10. Your output will be combined with nine other angles into a final report.
11. Focus ONLY on your assigned angle — do not duplicate other angles' work.
12. Length: aim for 800-1200 words of substantive content.

response_content:
# Angle 9: Table 3 Output Format and Validation Rules

This section formalises the structure of the required output table (Table 3) and the precise mathematical conditions that a feasible schedule must satisfy – conditions that can be verified by inspecting the rows of Table 3.  
The table is derived from an optimal (or feasible) solution of the two‑crew, multi‑workshop FJSSP with asynchronous operation‑level starts and early equipment release.

## 1. Notation and Underlying Solution Variables

We first define the sets and parameters that are needed to construct Table 3. The solution is assumed to provide a complete set of assignment and timing decisions.

**Workshops and processes**  
Let \(W=\{A,B,C,D,E\}\) be the set of workshops. Workshop C contains the expanded chain  
\(C1, C2, C3_1, C4_1, C5_1, C3_2, C4_2, C5_2, C3_3, C4_3, C5_3\).  
Denote by \(\mathcal{J}\) the set of all distinct processes across all workshops. For each \(j\in\mathcal{J}\) we write \(w(j)\in W\) for its workshop.

**Equipment types and units**  
\(T = \{\text{Automated Conveying Arm},\ \text{Industrial Cleaning Machine},\ \text{Precision Filling Machine},\ \text{Automatic Sensing Multi‑function Machine},\ \text{High‑speed Polishing Machine}\}\).  
For a process \(j\), \(E_j\subseteq T\) is the set of equipment types required (one or two).  
For each type \(t\in T\), \(U_t\) is the set of all equipment units of that type (from both crews) – 32 units in total. Every unit \(k\in U_t\) has a crew affiliation \(g(k)\in\{1,2\}\), directly readable from its name (e.g. the suffix “1‑3” or “2‑1”).

**Workload and processing durations**  
The processing time \(p_{j,t}\) (integer seconds) for operation \((j,t)\) is obtained by
\[
p_{j,t} = \Big\lceil \frac{\text{workload}(j)}{\text{efficiency}(t)} \times 3600 \Big\rceil .
\]
These values are known constants.

**Transport times**  
- For a unit \(k\) belonging to crew \(c=g(k)\), the travel time from its home base to workshop \(w\) is \(\tau_c(w) = \delta_c(w)/v\), where \(\delta_c(w)\) is the distance from crew‑\(c\) base to workshop \(w\) and \(v=2\,\text{m/s}\).
- Inter‑workshop travel time is \(\tau(w,w') = d(w,w')/v\) with symmetric distance matrix \(d(w,w')\); \(\tau(w,w)=0\).

**Decision variables (provided by the solver)**  

For each process \(j\) and each required equipment type \(t\in E_j\) we have:
- Assignment variable: \(A_{j,t,k} \in \{0,1\}\) with \(\sum_{k\in U_t} A_{j,t,k}=1\) (each operation gets exactly one unit).
- Start time variable: \(s_{j,t}\ge 0\) (continuous, integer seconds).
- End time: \(e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}\) (derived).
- Process completion time: \(PCT_j = \max_{t\in E_j} e_{j,t}\).

These variables exactly encode the flexible, asynchronous nature of the schedule.

## 2. Exact Format of Table 3

Table 3 contains one row for **each equipment‑operation assignment** – i.e. for every triple \((j,t,k)\) with \(A_{j,t,k}=1\).  
A process that requires two equipment types will therefore produce **two rows**, possibly with different Start and End times.

The columns are:

| Column | Content | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Equipment ID | e.g. “Automated Conveying Arm1‑3” | The unique identifier of the unit \(k\). |
| Start Time | `HH:MM:SS` format (e.g. `01:23:45`) | Integer seconds \(s_{j,t}\) converted to zero‑padded \(hh:mm:ss\). |
| End Time | `HH:MM:SS` | \(e_{j,t} = s_{j,t}+p_{j,t}\). |
| Continuous Operation Duration | Integer seconds \(p_{j,t}\) | Processing time of this single equipment unit. |
| Process ID | e.g. “A1”, “C3_2” | The process \(j\) that the operation belongs to. |
| Crew | `1` or `2` | Derived from \(g(k)\) (the unit’s affiliation). |

All time values are in whole seconds (ceiling‑rounded). The time origin is `00:00:00`. The table may be sorted in any convenient order, but most natural is per equipment unit.

## 3. Validation Rules

The following seven rules state the necessary and sufficient conditions for a schedule to be physically feasible. They must all hold for a valid Table 3, and they can be checked mechanically from the columns.

### Rule 1 – Assignment Correctness
Every operation \((j,t)\) appears in **exactly one row**.
\[
\forall j\in\mathcal{J},\; \forall t\in E_j:\quad \sum_{k\in U_t} A_{j,t,k} = 1.
\]
In the table, this means for each ordered pair \((j,t)\) there is precisely one row with that Process ID and with an Equipment ID of the correct type \(t\).

### Rule 2 – Asynchronous Starts Permitted
Rows belonging to the same process \(j\) are **not** required to have the same Start Time.
No mathematical equality is imposed between \(s_{j,t_1}\) and \(s_{j,t_2}\) for \(t_1\neq t_2\).  
The table may therefore legitimately show, for example, that the Automated Conveying Arm starts before the Precision Filling Machine for process A1, or vice versa.

### Rule 3 – Per‑Row Precedence (Workshop Order)
For every adjacent pair of processes \((pred, succ)\) inside a workshop, **each row of the successor process** must satisfy:
\[
\forall t\in E_{succ}:\quad s_{succ, t} \;\ge\; PCT_{pred}.
\]
Here \(PCT_{pred}\) is the maximum End Time among all rows of the predecessor process (see Rule 4).  
Equivalently, for every row with Process ID \(succ\), its Start Time column must be \(\ge\) the latest End Time of all rows of \(pred\).

### Rule 4 – PCT Consistency
For each process \(j\), the process completion time \(PCT_j\) is the **maximum** End Time over its rows:
\[
PCT_j = \max_{t\in E_j,\; k: A_{j,t,k}=1} e_{j,t}.
\]
Thus, looking at all rows sharing the same Process ID, \(PCT_j\) equals the largest End Time among them.

### Rule 5 – Equipment Non‑Overlap and Transport Gaps
For a fixed equipment unit \(k\), sort its rows by increasing Start Time.  
Let the resulting sequence be \((j_1,t_1), (j_2,t_2), \dots, (j_m,t_m)\).  
For every consecutive pair \((i,i+1)\) we require:
\[
s_{j_{i+1},t_{i+1}} \;\ge\; e_{j_i, t_i} \;+\; \tau\big(w(j_i),\, w(j_{i+1})\big).
\]
- If the two operations are in the same workshop, \(\tau=0\), so the next operation may start immediately after the previous one finishes (no overlap).
- If they are in different workshops, a non‑negative travel gap equal to the inter‑workshop transport time must be present.

In the table, for each Equipment ID, the rows’ time windows \([Start, End)\) must be disjoint and the gap between successive rows must cover at least the required travel time derived from the distance matrix.

### Rule 6 – Initial Transport from Crew Base
Each equipment unit \(k\) starts idle at its crew’s base at time 0. The first operation assigned to \(k\) (the one with the smallest Start Time among its rows) must satisfy:
\[
s_{\text{first}} \;\ge\; \tau_{g(k)}\big(w(\text{first})\big).
\]
In the table, for every equipment unit, the earliest row’s Start Time must be at least the travel time from the corresponding crew base to the workshop of that first process. This uses the crew‑specific initial distance \(\delta_{g(k)}(w)\).

### Rule 7 – Early Release Verification
Every row’s End Time is built as
\[
e_{j,t} = s_{j,t} + p_{j,t}.
\]
No row’s End Time is delayed to the \(PCT\) of its process. The “Continuous Operation Duration” column equals the difference between End Time and Start Time, irrespective of any other equipment used in the same process. This ensures that each piece of equipment is released immediately after finishing its own workload, verifying the early‑release principle.

A simple check: for any process with two rows, the two End Times may differ; the row with the earlier End Time must not have been held until the later End Time.

## 4. Completeness

The seven rules above fully characterise a feasible schedule for the two‑crew FJSSP with asynchronous starts and early release. When the solver produces the decision variables, the rows of Table 3 are generated as described, and every valid solution will automatically satisfy these conditions. Conversely, any table that passes these validation rules (with distances and processing times honoured) corresponds to a physically realisable schedule.
